article.title: Snap Cuts 1,000 Jobs and Credits AI for Enabling Leaner Teams as Activist Investor Pushes for Profitable Growth
article.summary: Snapchat's parent company eliminates 16% of its workforce, claiming AI now generates over 65% of new code, as Irenic Capital pressures the company to shed its AR hardware ambitions.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Snap, the parent company of Snapchat, is eliminating approximately 1,000 jobs and closing more than 300 open positions, reducing its full-time workforce by roughly 16%. CEO Evan Spiegel announced the cuts in an internal memo on April 15, citing rapid advances in artificial intelligence as a key enabler of smaller, more productive teams, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/15/snap-is-cutting-1000-jobs-16-of-its-workforce/).

The restructuring arrives weeks after activist investor Irenic Capital Management began pressing the company to improve its financial performance, and it marks Snap's fourth major round of layoffs in four years.

## What We Know

Snap had approximately 5,261 full-time employees as of December 31, 2025, according to [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/04/15/snap-to-cut-about-1000-jobs-or-16-of-its-global-workforce/). The latest cuts will bring the company's headcount below 4,300.

Spiegel framed the reductions as a direct consequence of AI-driven productivity gains. In his memo, he wrote that "rapid advancements in artificial intelligence enable our teams to reduce repetitive work, increase velocity, and better support our community," as reported by [Variety](https://variety.com/2026/digital/news/snap-layoffs-snapchat-ceo-evan-spiegel-cites-ai-1236722228/). The company claimed it has "already witnessed small squads leveraging AI tools to drive meaningful progress" on Snapchat+, its advertising platform, and Snap Lite infrastructure, according to [Variety](https://variety.com/2026/digital/news/snap-layoffs-snapchat-ceo-evan-spiegel-cites-ai-1236722228/).

One of the most striking claims in the announcement is that AI now generates more than 65% of new code at Snap, with the company increasingly assigning work to "focused teams and AI agents," according to [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/articles/snap-lay-off-16-staff-101450564.html).

Financially, Snap expects the layoffs to cut more than $500 million in annualized expenses by the second half of 2026, with severance and related charges projected at $95 million to $130 million, mostly in the second quarter, as reported by [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/articles/snap-lay-off-16-staff-101450564.html). Spiegel described the goal as establishing "a clearer path to net-income profitability," according to [Variety](https://variety.com/2026/digital/news/snap-layoffs-snapchat-ceo-evan-spiegel-cites-ai-1236722228/).

U.S.-based departing employees will receive four months of severance, continued healthcare coverage, equity vesting acceleration, and career transition support, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/04/15/snap-is-cutting-1000-jobs-16-of-its-workforce/).

Snap's stock rose approximately 5.8% following the announcement, though it remained down 31% year-to-date, according to [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/articles/snap-lay-off-16-staff-101450564.html).

## Activist Pressure and AR Hardware Questions

The layoffs follow a campaign by Irenic Capital Management, which holds a 2.5% economic interest in Snap, urging the company to optimize its portfolio and improve performance. Irenic has specifically pushed for Snap to spin off or shut down its Spectacles augmented reality glasses unit, which the investor says has consumed $3.5 billion in investment, according to [Yahoo Finance](https://finance.yahoo.com/markets/stocks/articles/snap-lay-off-16-staff-101450564.html).

Snap still plans to launch a new version of Spectacles in 2026, though the activist pressure raises questions about the unit's long-term viability within the company.

## A Pattern of Contraction

This is not Snap's first round of significant workforce reductions. The company cut 20% of its staff in 2022, followed by a 3% reduction in late 2023 and a 10% cut in 2024, according to [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/04/15/snap-to-cut-about-1000-jobs-or-16-of-its-global-workforce/). Despite repeated restructurings, Snap posted a net loss of $460 million in 2025 on revenue of $5.9 billion, according to [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/04/15/snap-to-cut-about-1000-jobs-or-16-of-its-global-workforce/).

As [previously reported](/article/2026-04/14-q1-2026-tech-layoffs-hit-80000-as-nearly-half-of-all-cuts-are-attributed-to-ai-but-the-real-picture-is-more-complicated), nearly half of all Q1 2026 tech layoffs have been attributed to AI, though the relationship between AI adoption and job cuts is often more complex than companies publicly acknowledge.

## What We Don't Know

Snap has not disclosed which departments or teams bear the brunt of the layoffs, nor has it detailed how the 65% AI-generated code figure was calculated or what quality and review processes govern that output. It remains unclear whether the company's AI productivity claims reflect genuine structural change or serve primarily as narrative framing for cost cuts driven by activist pressure and persistent losses.

Snapchat's user base stood at 946 million monthly active users and 474 million daily active users as of the end of 2025, according to [Variety](https://variety.com/2026/digital/news/snap-layoffs-snapchat-ceo-evan-spiegel-cites-ai-1236722228/) and [Fortune](https://fortune.com/2026/04/15/snap-to-cut-about-1000-jobs-or-16-of-its-global-workforce/) respectively, suggesting the product side remains healthy even as the company contracts its workforce. Snap's first-quarter results, expected on May 6, will offer the first concrete test of whether the leaner structure translates into improved margins.
